sentences.base_language: I went for a run this morning.
sentences.grammarNote:
**跑步了** — the **了** at the sentence end marks a completed action or new situation: 'I went for a run (and I did it).' It confirms the activity happened, often with a sense of reporting.
**跑步** (pǎobù) = to run / jogging. **跑** (run) + **步** (step). Compare **走路** (walk — 'walk steps'). Time expressions come before the verb in Chinese: **今天早上** (this morning) + verb.

Duration pattern: verb + **了** + time duration. **跑了三十分钟** = 'ran for thirty minutes'. If an object follows, add another **了**: **我跑了三十分钟的步** (I ran for thirty minutes of jogging).
**分钟** (fēnzhōng) = minute(s). **分** = minute/divide; **钟** = clock. For hours: **小时** (xiǎoshí, hour) or **钟头** (zhōngtóu, hour colloquial).

Body-part as topic: **我腿很酸** (my legs are sore) — the body part (**腿**) is the topic and comes right after the subject **我**. This topic-comment structure is very common in Chinese.
**酸** (suān) = sour/achy — used for the dull post-exercise muscle soreness that Chinese describes as 'sour'. **腿** contains the **月** (flesh) radical. **现在** (xiànzài) = now/at present — placed before the topic/subject.

**之后** (zhīhòu) = 'after'. More formal than **后** alone. **之前** (zhīqián) = 'before'. Pattern: action + **之后/之前** = after/before the action.
**应该** (yīnggāi) = should/ought to. Slightly stronger than **可以** (may) — implies what is advisable or proper. **拉伸** (lāshēn) = to stretch: **拉** (pull/stretch) + **伸** (extend).

sentences.base_language: I take a shower after exercising.
sentences.grammarNote:
**运动完** = 'finish exercising'. **完** (wán) as a resultative complement after a verb indicates completion. **运动完** → **做完** (finish doing), **吃完** (finish eating), **看完** (finish watching).
**洗澡** (xǐzǎo) = to shower/bathe. **洗** (wash) + **澡** (bath). Compare **洗脸** (wash face), **洗手** (wash hands) — **洗** + body/object forms many hygiene compounds.

**能** (néng) expresses capability or possibility: 'can / is able to'. Compare with **可以** (kěyǐ, permission/possibility) and **会** (huì, learned ability or prediction). **能** focuses on physical or situational ability.
**保持** (bǎochí) = to maintain/keep. **身体健康** = body healthy — **身体** is the object and **健康** is its complement (adjective), meaning 'the body (in a) healthy (state)'.

sentences.base_language: I try to exercise at least three times a week.
sentences.target_language: 我每周尽量锻炼至少三次。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ měi zhōu jǐnliàng duànliàn zhìshǎo sān cì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**尽量** (jǐnliàng) = 'as much as possible / try to'. **至少** (zhìshǎo) = 'at least'. **每周** = 'every week' (**每** + time unit). **次** (cì) = measure word for occurrences: **三次** = three times.
**锻炼** (duànliàn) = exercise/work out (more deliberate training than **运动**). **尽量** goes before the verb it modifies, as most adverbs do in Chinese.

sentences.romanized: Wǒ jīntiān shēntǐ gǎnjué hěn hǎo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**身体感觉很好** — **身体** (body) is the topic after the subject **我**: 'I, [as for my] body, feel very good'. This double-topic structure (subject + body part topic + comment) is natural in Chinese.
**感觉** (gǎnjué) = feel/feeling. Can function as verb or noun: **感觉很好** (feels great) or **这种感觉很好** (this feeling is great). **今天** (jīntiān) = today — time word precedes the topic.
